text.title: Secondary Quality Attributes: Security, Maintainability, Testability, Observability
text.summary: Definitions and architectural tactics for the four secondary-but-critical quality attributes. Security and Observability are often underprioritized until an incident occurs.

rule.statement: Apply the CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) as a checklist to every data asset and API endpoint. Threat-model during architecture design, not after first deployment. Document the auth mechanism chosen in an ADR.
rule.rationale: Security retrofitted after architecture is frozen is expensive and incomplete. Threat modeling during design costs hours; fixing a deployed vulnerability costs weeks plus incident response overhead.

list.item: Confidentiality: only authorized users can read the data — encryption at rest and in transit, RBAC, column-level permissions
Integrity: data cannot be modified without authorization — input validation, digital signatures, audit logs
Availability: service available despite attack — rate limiting, DDoS mitigation, circuit breakers
Auth patterns: OAuth 2.0 + OIDC for web APIs; API keys for service-to-service; mTLS inside the cluster
OWASP Top 10: injection, broken auth, insecure deserialization — validate and sanitize all input at the boundary

rule.statement: Measure maintainability by cycle time: how long does it take a new developer to make a change in an unfamiliar module? Target: <1 day to make a scoped change without breaking other modules. Apply SOLID at module boundaries, not mechanically at every class.
rule.rationale: Abstract maintainability metrics (cyclomatic complexity, lines of code) correlate weakly with actual change cost. Cycle time for scoped changes is the real proxy: it includes test time, review time, and deploy confidence.

list.item: Modularity: low coupling between modules, high cohesion within — testable modules with clear boundaries
Reusability: shared behavior in libraries, not copy-pasted — single source of truth for domain rules
Analyzability: code is readable, errors produce informative messages — structured logging with correlation IDs
Modifiability: changes have limited blast radius — use contracts/interfaces at module boundaries

rule.statement: Design for testability by ensuring each layer can be tested in isolation. The test pyramid: 70% unit tests (fast, isolated), 20% integration tests (real DB/broker), 10% E2E tests (slow, fragile). Invert dependencies so domain logic can be tested without infrastructure.
rule.rationale: Systems not designed for testability require a real running database for every test, making the test suite slow and flaky. Dependency inversion (ports and adapters) lets the entire domain layer be tested with in-memory fakes.

list.item: Unit: isolated, no I/O, milliseconds — test domain logic and use cases with fakes
Integration: real database or broker, seconds — test repository implementations and adapters
E2E: full stack, minutes — test critical user journeys only
Contract tests: verify API producer/consumer compatibility without integration test coupling

rule.statement: Implement the three observability pillars — logs, metrics, traces — from day one. A system is observable if an engineer can answer "why is the p99 latency spiking right now?" from telemetry alone, without touching production code.
rule.rationale: Observability is the ability to understand internal system state from external outputs. Without structured logs and distributed traces, debugging production incidents requires guesswork or attaching debuggers to live systems — both unacceptable in production.

list.item: Logs: structured JSON with correlation IDs; include request_id, user_id, service, level, message
Metrics: USE (Utilization/Saturation/Errors) for resources; RED (Rate/Errors/Duration) for services
Traces: distributed trace per request spanning all services — OpenTelemetry is the standard instrumentation library
Alerting: alert on SLO breach, not on individual metrics — reduces alert fatigue
